edges.description: ZBTB20 transcriptional dysfunction dysregulates neurodevelopmental gene programs.
ZBTB20 transcriptional dysfunction dysregulates growth and metabolic gene programs.

edges.target: Calcification of the External Ear Cartilage

nodes.description: Autism spectrum / autistic behavior reported in roughly 61% of cohort patients.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: cognitive deficit associated with autism spectrum disorder

nodes.description: Behavioral issues are characteristic and can include severe neurobehavioral dysregulation (aggression, irritability, mood lability) in adolescence.

evidence.reference_title: Resolution of severe neurobehavioral difficulties in an individual with Primrose syndrome with sertraline.
evidence.snippet: Neurobehavioral difficulties included aggression towards self and others, irritability, tearfulness, and mood liability that did not respond to behavioral interventions or aripiprazole.

nodes.description: Calcification (and ossification) of the external ear cartilage is a cardinal progressive sign, typically emerging in later childhood/adolescence; present in essentially all assessed adults. The best-fit HPO term is the broader "Calcification of cartilage"; the preferred term captures the ear-specific localization.
nodes.id: Calcification of the External Ear Cartilage

evidence.reference_title: Primrose Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Radiographic features include calcification of the external ear cartilage, multiple wormian bones, platybasia, bathrocephaly, slender bones with exaggerated metaphyseal flaring, mild epiphyseal dysplasia, and spondylar dysplasia.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: the cardinal features, such as calcification of the external ears, cystic bone lesions, muscle wasting, and contractures typically develop between 10 and 16 years of age.

nodes.description: Cataract is an age-related feature found in adults with Primrose syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Dysplastic hip joint changes, cystic bone lesions, and cataract were found only in adults.

nodes.description: Corpus callosum anomalies (dysgenesis/agenesis) are seen on brain MRI, significantly more frequently in patients with missense variants.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: a phenotypic comparison of patients with a ZBTB20 missense variant versus a 3q13.31 microdeletion including ZBTB20.
evidence.snippet: Corpus callosum dysgenesis

evidence.reference_title: An Infant With Primrose Syndrome: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: an absent corpus callosum observed on postnatal MRI and genotypic findings of a pathogenic variant in ZBTB20.

nodes.description: Cryptorchidism is reported among affected males.

evidence.reference_title: A Novel ZBTB20 Variant in a Patient with Primrose Syndrome: A Rare Clinical Entity with Distinctive Features.
evidence.snippet: Among other characteristic findings are ocular abnormalities, hearing loss, calcification of the external ear cartilage, nonspecific brain magnetic resonance imaging findings, and cryptorchidism.

nodes.description: Deeply set eyes are a recurring component of the Primrose syndrome facial gestalt.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: unusual facial features (frontal bossing, deeply set eyes, down‐slanting palpebral fissures)

nodes.description: Disturbed glucose metabolism is a cardinal biochemical feature; adults may develop insulin-resistant diabetes requiring oral hypoglycemics and/or insulin.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and functional characterization of two novel ZBTB20 mutations causing Primrose syndrome.
evidence.snippet: disturbances of glucose metabolism with insulin-resistant diabetes and distal muscle wasting occurring in adulthood.

nodes.description: Progressive distal muscle wasting, first noticed around age 11 years and increasing with age; a muscle biopsy in one patient demonstrated neurogenic atrophy.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Muscle wasting was first noticed at age 11

nodes.description: ZBTB20 acts as a regulator of somatic growth, fetal liver development, detoxification, and glucose metabolism. Its dysregulation in Primrose syndrome is linked to postnatal overgrowth (macrocephaly, with variable tall stature), disturbed glucose metabolism with insulin-resistant diabetes, and a metabolic signature suggesting disturbed mitochondrial fatty-acid oxidation (abnormal acylcarnitines and urine organic acids). ZBTB20 normally represses hepatic alpha-fetoprotein expression, and loss of this repression is proposed to underlie the characteristically elevated serum AFP.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: ZBTB20 acts as a regulator of neurogenesis, fetal liver development, somatic growth, detoxification and glucose metabolism.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Biochemically, unexplained anemia, disturbed glucose metabolism, and increased AFP levels are cardinal features of PS.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and functional characterization of two novel ZBTB20 mutations causing Primrose syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The disorder is caused by functional dysregulation of ZBTB20, a transcriptional repressor controlling energetic metabolism and developmental programs.

nodes.description: Narrow, frequently downslanting palpebral fissures are part of the craniofacial pattern.

evidence.reference_title: A Novel ZBTB20 Variant in a Patient with Primrose Syndrome: A Rare Clinical Entity with Distinctive Features.
evidence.snippet: a recognizable facial phenotype including deep set eyes, ptosis, narrow and frequently downslanting palpebral fissures, and depressed nasal bridge.

nodes.description: ZBTB20 is a key regulator of neurogenesis, cortical lamination, and hippocampal development. Neurodevelopmental-disorder-associated ZBTB20 variants alter dendritic spine morphology, dendritic arborization, and dendritic length in cultured cortical pyramidal neurons, providing a plausible cellular substrate for the intellectual disability, autistic behavior, and behavioral phenotype of Primrose syndrome. Brain MRI commonly shows corpus callosum anomalies, particularly in patients with missense variants.

evidence.reference_title: Neurodevelopmental disorder-associated ZBTB20 gene variants affect dendritic and synaptic structure.
evidence.snippet: defects of ZBTB20, a BTB and zinc finger domain containing transcriptional repressor, have been implicated in a wide range of neurodevelopmental disorders, including intellectual disability and autism.

evidence.reference_title: Neurodevelopmental disorder-associated ZBTB20 gene variants affect dendritic and synaptic structure.
evidence.snippet: a single nucleotide variant identified in patients with intellectual disability and located at the C-terminus of ZBTB20 affected dendritic arborization and dendritic length but had no effect on dendritic spine morphology.

nodes.description: Intellectual disability is characteristically accompanied by delayed expressive speech development.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Primrose syndrome is characterized by macrocephaly, hypotonia, developmental delay, intellectual disability with expressive speech delay, behavioral issues, a recognizable facial phenotype, radiographic features, and altered glucose metabolism.

nodes.description: Part of the recognizable craniofacial gestalt, together with deeply set eyes, ptosis, downslanting palpebral fissures, and large ears.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: unusual facial features (frontal bossing, deeply set eyes, down‐slanting palpebral fissures)

nodes.description: Developmental delay including expressive speech delay; all 57 patients in a comparative review had intellectual disability and/or psychomotor delay.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: a phenotypic comparison of patients with a ZBTB20 missense variant versus a 3q13.31 microdeletion including ZBTB20.
evidence.snippet: All 57 patients presented mild-to-severe ID and/or a psychomotor delay.

nodes.description: Hypothyroidism occurs in a subset of patients and is enriched in the missense-variant group.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: a phenotypic comparison of patients with a ZBTB20 missense variant versus a 3q13.31 microdeletion including ZBTB20.
evidence.snippet: hypothyroidism (p = 0.047), and diabetes were also more frequent in this group.

nodes.description: Hypotonia, typically of infantile/childhood onset, present in ~76% of cohort patients and contributing to motor delay.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Primrose syndrome is characterized by macrocephaly, hypotonia, developmental delay, intellectual disability with expressive speech delay, behavioral issues, a recognizable facial phenotype, radiographic features, and altered glucose metabolism.

nodes.description: Moderate-to-severe intellectual disability in most patients (moderate-severe in ~84%, mild in ~16%); cognition does not appear to decline with age.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: The cardinal findings of PS are the ID (mild 16%, moderate‐severe 84%)

nodes.description: Joint contractures, typically beginning around age 10 years and becoming more prominent with age, can contribute to mobility disability.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Contractures were first noticed at age 10 years and became more prominent with age as well.

nodes.description: Postnatal macrocephaly / macrocrania reflecting non-progressive brain overgrowth, present in roughly three-quarters of patients.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Primrose syndrome (PS; MIM# 259050) is characterized by intellectual disability (ID), macrocephaly, unusual facial features (frontal bossing, deeply set eyes, down‐slanting palpebral fissures), calcified external ears, sparse body hair and distal muscle wasting.

nodes.description: Ptosis is part of the characteristic facial gestalt, reported in ~71% of cohort patients.

evidence.reference_title: A Novel ZBTB20 Variant in a Patient with Primrose Syndrome: A Rare Clinical Entity with Distinctive Features.
evidence.snippet: a recognizable facial phenotype including deep set eyes, ptosis, narrow and frequently downslanting palpebral fissures, and depressed nasal bridge.

nodes.description: Seizures occur in a subset of patients (~21% in the 42-patient cohort).

evidence.reference_title: Primrose Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: standard treatment for seizures, musculoskeletal issues, hearing loss, and thyroid dysfunction

nodes.description: Hearing loss, mostly sensorineural, is common in both children and adults and is significantly more frequent in patients with missense variants than those with deletions/truncating variants.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Hearing loss is also common both in children and adults, mostly presenting as sensorineural hearing loss.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: a phenotypic comparison of patients with a ZBTB20 missense variant versus a 3q13.31 microdeletion including ZBTB20.
evidence.snippet: Hearing loss was far more frequent in patients with missense SNVs (p = 0.002)

nodes.description: Sparse body hair is a characteristic feature, most recognizable in adults.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: sparse body hair, distal muscle wasting, and contractures

nodes.description: Increased postnatal growth in height; present in a minority of patients and more marked in males, where growth may exceed the 98th centile.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in ZBTB20 cause Primrose syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Primrose syndrome and 3q13.31 microdeletion syndrome are clinically related disorders characterized by tall stature, macrocephaly, intellectual disability, disturbed behavior and unusual facial features, with diabetes, deafness, progressive muscle wasting and ectopic calcifications specifically occurring in the former.

nodes.description: Adult males with Primrose syndrome are at elevated risk of testicular germ cell tumors. This is a prognosis-affecting complication: both reported affected males who developed testicular tumors died of their tumors, prompting recommendations for surveillance in adult males.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Two adult males with PS developed a testicular tumor.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: The two males with PS who developed testicular tumors have died because of their tumors.

nodes.description: ZBTB20 is a BTB-zinc finger (BTB-ZF) transcriptional repressor with an N-terminal BTB domain mediating protein-protein interactions and five C-terminal C2H2 zinc fingers mediating sequence-specific DNA binding to promoters of target genes. Most Primrose syndrome variants are de novo missense changes affecting the first and second zinc-finger motifs, which disrupt DNA binding. Functional studies show that both missense and truncating Primrose-causing variants exert a dominant-negative impact on wild-type ZBTB20, interfering with normal transcriptional repression and dysregulating downstream developmental, growth, and metabolic gene programs.

biological_processes: Negative regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase II

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: The protein is a member of the broad complex tramtrack bric‐a‐brac (BTB) zinc‐finger (ZnF) family and is characterized by an N‐terminal BTB domain that is involved in protein‐protein interaction, and five C2H2 zinc fingers at the C‐terminus mediating protein binding to regulatory sites within promoters of target genes.

evidence.reference_title: Primrose syndrome: Characterization of the phenotype in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: all ZBTB20 variants causing PS have been missense variants that affect amino acid residues in the first and second ZnF motifs.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and functional characterization of two novel ZBTB20 mutations causing Primrose syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Our data document that both mutations have dominant negative impact on wild-type ZBTB20, providing further evidence of the specific behavior of PS-causing mutations on ZBTB20 function.
